title: Borrowing & credit: the real price of 'buy now'
description: Borrow like you've read the fine print — because you have.
summaryPoints: Interest is the rent you pay on borrowed money — APR is its yearly price
Pay your card in full each month and interest is $0; carry a balance and it's around 20–25% APR
Minimum payments are the slowest, priciest way out — $1,000 can become ~$1,450
Payday loans run around 390% APR, and rollovers are the trap
Snowball = smallest balance first; avalanche = highest APR first

content.title: Borrowed money is rented money
content.body:
Someday you'll want something big — a car, a degree, maybe a home — before you've saved the full price. That's what borrowing is for, and it isn't evil. It's a tool.

But borrowed money is rented money. The rent is called **interest**, and the lender always collects.

Master one question before borrowing anything: "What will this REALLY cost me by the end?"

content.title: The borrowing toolkit
content.body:
- **Credit card** — borrow up to a limit, repay monthly. Pay the FULL balance and interest is $0. Carry a balance and you pay around 20–25% APR (typical card rates)
- **Loans** — student loans, car loans, mortgages. Borrowed once, repaid in installments
- **APR** — the yearly price of borrowing. Borrow $1,000 at 10% APR and you owe about $100 in interest per year

Higher APR = more expensive money. Always check it before you sign.

content.prompt: Match each way of borrowing to what it really is.

pairs.right: Pays for school, often lower APR

content.prompt: You borrow $1,000 at 10% APR. After one year, the interest comes to $___.
content.explanation: APR is the yearly price of borrowing: 10% of $1,000 = $100 in interest per year.

content.prompt: If you pay your credit card's full balance every month, using it costs you $0 in interest.
content.explanation: That's the cheat code: use the card, pay in full every month, build history — and never pay the 20–25% APR.

content.title: The minimum-payment trap
content.body:
Leila buys a $1,000 laptop on her credit card.

Pays it off that month? Total cost: $1,000. Done.

Pays only the $30 minimum at 20% APR? The unpaid balance keeps growing interest. Clearing it takes about four years, and the laptop ends up costing roughly $1,450.

Minimum payments are designed to keep you in debt comfortably — not to get you out. The card company profits from every month you stay.
content.imageAlt: A spiral showing a balance shrinking slowly under minimum payments

content.prompt: What's the cheapest way to get it?
content.context: You want a $1,000 laptop for school. You have no savings yet, but you can set aside $200 a month.
options: Buy it now on a credit card and pay the $30 minimum
Take a payday loan for the full $1,000
Save $200 a month and buy it in 5 months
Take a 5% student loan and repay it over a year
content.explanation: Saving costs zero interest — just patience. The 5% loan is a decent runner-up; minimum payments quietly turn $1,000 into roughly $1,450.

content.instructions: One word turns this sentence into a lie. Tap it.
content.sentence: Paying only the minimum is the fastest way to clear a credit card.
content.explanation: Minimum payments mostly cover interest, so the balance barely moves. It's the slowest — and most expensive — way out.

content.title: Payday loans: the quicksand
content.body:
Payday loans hand you cash fast — at brutal prices.

Karim borrows $500. The fee: $75 every two weeks. He can't repay in time, so he rolls it over… four times. Two months later he owes $800 for a $500 loan. That pace works out to roughly 390% APR.

Compare that to a typical credit card (20–25% APR) or a student loan (~5%) and the trap is obvious: payday lenders profit most when you CAN'T pay them back.

content.prompt: Karim borrows $500 at a $75 fee per two weeks, then rolls it over for two months — four fee cycles. Slide to what he owes in total.
content.explanation: $500 + (4 × $75) = $800. Each rollover adds another fee while the original debt never shrinks.

content.context: Your car needs a $200 repair today. You have no savings, but you do have a credit card and a paycheck coming in three weeks.
options: Charge $200 to the card and pay it off in full next bill
Payday loan: $200 now, $250 due in two weeks
Skip the repair and hope the noise stops
content.explanation: Pay the card in full when the bill arrives and the interest is $0. The payday loan charges $50 for two weeks — and gets uglier if you slip.

content.title: Two ways out of debt
content.body:
Got several debts? Pick a strategy:
- **Snowball** — pay minimums on everything, then throw every spare dollar at the SMALLEST BALANCE first. Quick wins keep you motivated
- **Avalanche** — same setup, but target the HIGHEST APR first. Saves the most money in interest

Say you owe: store card $200 at 22%, credit card $500 at 27%, student loan $1,000 at 5%.

Snowball attacks the $200 store card. Avalanche attacks the 27% credit card. Either beats paying randomly.

content.prompt: You're using the AVALANCHE method. Which debt gets every spare dollar first?
content.context: Your debts: store card $200 at 22% APR, credit card $500 at 27% APR, student loan $1,000 at 5% APR.
content.explanation: Avalanche targets the highest APR — the 27% card — to save the most interest. (Snowball would pick the $200 store card: smallest balance.)

content.prompt: Build a debt payoff plan. Put the steps in order.
steps: List every debt with its balance and APR
Choose your strategy: snowball or avalanche
Keep paying the minimum on every debt
Throw every spare dollar at your target debt
content.explanation: Know what you owe, pick a target, protect yourself from late fees — then attack one debt at a time until it's gone.

items.text: Paying your card in full every month
Checking the APR before you sign
Paying only the minimum for years
A 5% student loan for a degree

content.title: How to smell a debt trap
content.body:
Predatory lenders advertise everywhere — DMs, ads, flyers near campus. The tells:
- **"No credit check!"** — real lenders check whether you can repay
- **Fees instead of APR** — "$75 per two weeks" sounds small; as a yearly rate it's gigantic
- **Rollovers offered upfront** — they're planning for you to get stuck
- **Pressure to act NOW** — good deals survive a day of thinking

Let's see if you can spot them in the wild.

content.prompt: This ad slid into your DMs. Tap all 4 red flags.

segments.text: Hey friend! Tight week? Good news —
you're PRE-APPROVED for $500. No credit check, no questions asked!
segments.flag: Real lenders check whether you can repay. 'No credit check' means the trap IS the business model.

segments.text: Cash hits your account in 10 minutes.
Just a small $75 service fee every two weeks until you repay.
segments.flag: $75 per two weeks on $500 is roughly 390% APR. 'Small fee' is doing a lot of lying here.

segments.text: Can't pay yet? No stress — roll it over as many times as you like!
segments.flag: Rollovers stack fee after fee while the debt never shrinks. That's the quicksand.

segments.text: Offer expires at MIDNIGHT — claim your cash now!
segments.flag: Fake urgency exists to stop you from thinking. A real deal survives a night's sleep.

content.explanation: Need cash fast? A payment plan, extra shifts, or a credit card paid in full next month all beat 390% APR.
